meta.description: SEA Regional Language Knowledge Base for Kura AI — covers 22 regional languages across 11 ASEAN countries

common_phrases: Apa kaba? — How are you?
Kaba baik — I'm fine
Dari mano ang? — Where are you from?
Namonyo siapo? — What is your name?
Ambo indak mangarati — I don't understand
Tolong ulang lai — Please repeat that
Bara haragonyo? — How much does it cost?
Urang awak — Fellow Minangkabau person (expression of solidarity)
Minangkabau.grammar_notes: Minangkabau uses Subject-Verb-Object order. Politeness is expressed through vocabulary choice rather than grammatical inflection. The language has a rich oral tradition through 'petatah-petitih' (proverbs).
Minangkabau.cultural_notes: Minangkabau is the world's largest matrilineal society. Land and property pass through the female line. The iconic Rumah Gadang (big house) has distinctive curved rooftops. Famous for the Rendang dish and the Merantau (migration) tradition.
threats: Dominance of Indonesian (Bahasa Indonesia) in education and media
Urbanisation causing younger generations to shift to Indonesian
Limited digital content in Minangkabau language
Declining use within families
preservation_efforts: Local schools in West Sumatra teach Minangkabau as a subject
Minangkabau cultural festivals (e.g., Tabuik)
Online communities and YouTube channels in Minang language
Petatah-petitih (proverb) documentation projects

Jawa.script: Latin and Javanese script (Aksara Jawa / Hanacaraka)

speech_levels.Ngoko: Informal — used with close friends and family or younger people
speech_levels.Madya: Semi-formal — polite but not overly formal
speech_levels.Krama: Formal — used with elders, superiors, and strangers

common_phrases: Piye kabare? — How are you? (informal)
Kabare sae? — How are you? (formal)
Saking pundi? — Where are you from? (formal)
Jenenge sopo? — What is your name? (informal)
Kula mboten mangertos — I don't understand
Matur nuwun sanget — Thank you very much
Nyuwun sewu — Excuse me / I beg your pardon
Jawa.grammar_notes: Javanese has a complex speech level system (Unggah-ungguh) that reflects social hierarchy. The same sentence can be expressed entirely differently depending on the relationship between speakers. Verb forms and pronouns change with speech level.
Jawa.cultural_notes: Javanese culture is deeply influenced by Hindu-Buddhist and Islamic traditions. Concepts like 'rukun' (harmony) and 'hormat' (respect) are central. The Wayang (shadow puppet) tradition is a UNESCO heritage. Batik cloth originated from Javanese culture.
threats: Indonesian language dominance in all formal settings
Youth preferring Indonesian or English
Simplified Javanese being used, eroding the speech level system
Limited Javanese media content
preservation_efforts: Javanese language education in Central Java and Yogyakarta schools
Wayang and Gamelan traditions kept alive through cultural centers
Javanese literature and manuscript digitisation projects
Aksara Jawa script revival programs

common_phrases: Kumaha damang? — How are you? (formal)
Kumaha ieu? — How are you? (informal)
Ti mana? — Where are you from?
Saha nami anjeun? — What is your name?
Abdi teu ngartos — I don't understand
Hapunten — Excuse me / Sorry
Sunda.grammar_notes: Sundanese also has speech levels (Undak Usuk Basa): Kasar (rough), Sedeng (middle), and Lemes (refined). The 'Sampurasun — Rampes' greeting is unique and deeply cultural.
Sunda.cultural_notes: Sundanese people are known for the Angklung (bamboo musical instrument, UNESCO heritage) and Jaipongan dance. The Sunda Wiwitan (indigenous belief) and strong Islamic traditions coexist in Sundanese culture.
threats: Rapid urbanisation in the Greater Jakarta area absorbing Sundanese regions
Bahasa Indonesia replacing Sundanese in daily communication
Young generation rarely uses the formal Lemes speech level
Sundanese script rarely used or taught
preservation_efforts: Sundanese language taught in West Java schools
Angklung international promotion by UNESCO
Sundanese cultural festivals and Sisingaan performances
Digital Sundanese dictionary and media content

common_phrases: Sabai dee bor? — How are you?
Sabai dee — I'm fine
Jao maa tae sai? — Where are you from?
Jao seu nyang? — What is your name?
Bor khao jai — I don't understand
Khawp jai lai lai — Thank you very much
Shan.grammar_notes: Shan is a tonal language with 5 tones. It is closely related to Thai and Lao. Word order is Subject-Verb-Object. Nouns are not inflected for number or gender.
Shan.cultural_notes: The Shan people have a rich Buddhist tradition. The Shan New Year (Poi Sang Long) features young boys being ordained as novice monks. Shan literature and poetry have a long history. Shan State is known for its mountainous landscape and diverse ethnic groups.
threats: Burmese (Myanmar) language dominance in education and government
Political conflict in Shan State displacing communities
Limited formal education in Shan language
Young people migrating to cities and adopting Burmese
preservation_efforts: Shan language schools run by community organisations
Shan literature festivals and poetry competitions
Cross-border cultural connections with Tai peoples in Thailand and China
Digital Shan language resources and apps

Karen.speakers: ~3 million (Sgaw Karen, the most spoken variety)
Karen.script: Karen script (Sgaw Karen uses a script derived from Burmese)
common_phrases: Naw ka pwa? — How are you? (to a female)
Taw ka pwa? — How are you? (to a male)
Ka pwa — I'm fine
Baw thu — I don't know
Thu lay blu — Thank you
Karen.grammar_notes: Karen languages use a Subject-Object-Verb order, unlike most SEA languages. There are many Karen dialects — Sgaw and Pwo are the most spoken. Pronouns differ based on the speaker's gender.
Karen.cultural_notes: Karen people are predominantly Christian (Baptist) or Buddhist. The Karen New Year is celebrated in December. They are known for intricate hand-woven textiles. The Karen long-neck women (Kayan) are a sub-group famous globally.
threats: Decades of conflict in Myanmar causing displacement and language disruption
Refugee communities in Thailand face assimilation pressure
Limited access to education in Karen language
Many dialects with limited mutual intelligibility, making standardisation difficult
preservation_efforts: Karen language schools in refugee camps along Thai-Myanmar border
Karen Baptist Convention promoting literacy
Cultural preservation through traditional weaving and music
NGO-supported mother tongue education programs

common_phrases: Sabai dee bor? — How are you?
Khoi sabai dee — I'm fine
Jao ma tae sai? — Where are you from?
Jao seu nyang? — What is your name?
Khoi bor khao jai — I don't understand
Khawp jai lai lai — Thank you very much
Phaeng pao dai? — How much does it cost?
Lao.grammar_notes: Lao is a tonal language with 6 tones. It is closely related to Thai — speakers of both languages can often understand each other. Word order is Subject-Verb-Object. Lao script is written left to right with no spaces between words.
Lao.cultural_notes: Laos is a predominantly Buddhist country. The Boun Pi Mai (Lao New Year / Water Festival) in April is the biggest celebration. Sticky rice (khao niao) is the staple food and a cultural symbol. The Mekong River is central to Lao culture and livelihoods.
threats: Influence of Thai media (TV, music) causing language mixing
English growing in urban and tourist areas
Many ethnic minority languages within Laos competing
Youth preferring Thai pop culture
preservation_efforts: Lao script and language are taught in all national schools
Government promotion of Lao national identity
Cultural festivals preserving traditional music and dance
Lao literature and poetry traditions

Hmong.region: Northern highlands of Laos and Vietnam
common_phrases: Koj nyob li cas? — How are you?
Kuv nyob zoo — I'm fine
Koj lub npe hu li cas? — What is your name?
Kuv tsis to taub — I don't understand
Ua tsaug ntau — Thank you very much
Koj tuaj qhov twg los? — Where are you from?
Hmong.grammar_notes: Hmong is a tonal language with 8 tones (marked with final consonant letters in RPA). Word order is Subject-Verb-Object. There are many Hmong dialects — White Hmong (Hmong Daw) and Green Hmong (Mong Leng) are the most common.
Hmong.cultural_notes: Hmong people have a strong oral tradition with rich folktales and storytelling. The Paj Ntaub (flower cloth) embroidery is a cultural art form. Hmong New Year (Noj Peb Caug) is celebrated in November-December. Shamanism (Txiv Neeb) is a traditional spiritual practice.
threats: Lack of formal education in Hmong language
Government assimilation policies in Laos, Vietnam, and China
Displacement from highland communities to lowlands
Diaspora communities (USA, France, Australia) shifting to host country languages
preservation_efforts: Hmong diaspora communities maintaining language schools
RPA script enabling literacy and written Hmong material
NGOs supporting mother-tongue education in Hmong communities
Cultural festivals and oral tradition documentation

Isan.local_name: ภาษาอีสาน (Phasa Isan)
Isan.status: Vulnerable — being replaced by Central Thai
common_phrases: Sabai dee bor? — How are you?
Pen yang dai? — How's it going?
Ma tae sai? — Where are you from?
Seu nyang? — What is your name?
Bor khao jai — I don't understand
Si kan mai — See you again
Isan.grammar_notes: Isan is mutually intelligible with Lao. It uses the Thai script but with Lao pronunciation and vocabulary. It differs significantly from Central Thai in vocabulary and tone patterns.
Isan.cultural_notes: Isan people have a distinct cultural identity from Central Thailand. Mor lam (traditional singing) and khaen (bamboo mouth organ) music are iconic. Sticky rice is the staple, unlike jasmine rice in Central Thailand. Isan cuisine (e.g., som tam papaya salad, larb) is popular across Thailand.
threats: Central Thai dominance in all official, educational, and media contexts
Social stigma historically associated with Isan dialect
Urban migration causing language shift
Young Isan people mixing Central Thai vocabulary
preservation_efforts: Mor lam music keeping the language alive in popular culture
Isan cultural pride movements and festivals
Academic research on Isan linguistics
Cross-border cultural connections with Laos

Lanna.local_name: ภาษาเมือง / Kham Mueang
Lanna.region: Northern Thailand (Chiang Mai, Chiang Rai, Lampang, etc.)
Lanna.script: Tham script (ancient Lanna script) — rarely used today
common_phrases: Sabai dee ga? — How are you?
Khoi sabai dee — I'm fine
Ma tae sai? — Where are you from?
Seu nyang? — What is your name?
Bor khao jai — I don't understand
Muang nua — The North (Northern Thailand)
Lanna.grammar_notes: Lanna (Kham Mueang) is related to Central Thai but has distinct vocabulary, tones, and expressions. It has its own ancient script (Tham script) used historically for Buddhist texts.
Lanna.cultural_notes: Northern Thailand (Lanna Kingdom) has a distinct culture from Central Thailand. Yi Peng (Lantern Festival) is a famous Lanna tradition where thousands of lanterns are released into the sky during Loi Krathong season. Ancient Lanna temples (Wat Chedi Luang, Doi Suthep) reflect a unique architectural style.
threats: Central Thai dominance in all official contexts
Tourism industry promoting Central Thai as the national identity
Lanna script no longer widely taught
Young generation rarely speaks Kham Mueang
preservation_efforts: Lanna cultural centres and museums in Chiang Mai
Yi Peng festival preserving cultural traditions
Academic study of Tham script at Chiang Mai University
Community radio programs in Kham Mueang

Tay.region: Northeastern highlands (Cao Bang, Lang Son, Bac Kan, Thai Nguyen)
common_phrases: Slao mi tốt bấu? — How are you?
Kin tốt — I'm fine
Mừa pi tàng lẹ? — Where are you from?
Bấu rú — I don't understand
Khốp chjai — Thank you
Tay.grammar_notes: Tay is closely related to Lao and Shan. It has been heavily influenced by Vietnamese in vocabulary and phonology. Tay uses a tonal system similar to other Tai-Kadai languages.
Tay.cultural_notes: Tay people are the largest ethnic minority group in Vietnam. They have a rich tradition of Then singing (ritual music) which is a UNESCO intangible heritage. Tay cuisine features sticky rice, bamboo shoots, and grilled meats.
threats: Vietnamese dominance in all education and official life
Rapid integration with Vietnamese mainstream culture
Young generation shifting to Vietnamese as primary language
Limited written material and digital content
preservation_efforts: Then singing recognised as UNESCO intangible cultural heritage (2019)
Ethnic minority schools teaching Tay language in northern provinces
Vietnamese government ethnic minority preservation programs
Academic documentation of Tay oral literature

Khmer Krom.local_name: ភាសាខ្មែរក្រោម
Khmer Krom.region: Soc Trang, Tra Vinh, Kien Giang provinces
common_phrases: Chumreap suor — Hello (formal greeting with hands pressed together)
Neak sok sabay te? — How are you?
Knhom sok sabay — I'm fine
Neak mok pi na? — Where are you from?
Knhom min yul te — I don't understand
Aw kun — Thank you
Khmer Krom.grammar_notes: Khmer Krom is a dialect of standard Khmer (Cambodian). It has been influenced by Vietnamese in phonology and vocabulary. Khmer uses an abugida script with no spaces between words. Word order is Subject-Verb-Object.
Khmer Krom.cultural_notes: Khmer Krom people maintain strong Theravada Buddhist traditions. The Khmer Krom have historically lived in the Mekong Delta (historically called Kampuchea Krom) before it became part of Vietnam. They maintain unique festivals and pagoda-centred community life.
threats: Vietnamese dominance in education — Khmer Krom children educated solely in Vietnamese
Pressure to assimilate into Vietnamese mainstream culture
Limited rights for Khmer Krom cultural expression
Younger generation losing Khmer script literacy
preservation_efforts: Buddhist pagodas serving as cultural and language preservation centres
Khmer Krom diaspora communities in Cambodia and USA advocating for rights
Community-run Khmer language classes at pagodas
Cultural documentation by human rights organisations

Cham.region: Central Vietnam (Ninh Thuan, Binh Thuan), Cambodia (Phnom Penh region)
Cham.script: Cham script (ancient), Arabic script (Jawi for Muslim Cham)
common_phrases: Thau paran di? — How are you?
Kau saong — I'm fine
Kau ba kayua — I don't understand
Takai — Thank you
Ih mai tuk di? — Where are you from?
Cham.grammar_notes: Cham is an Austronesian language, making it related to Malay and Indonesian — unusual for mainland Southeast Asia. It has Subject-Verb-Object word order. There are two main groups: Eastern Cham (Vietnam) and Western Cham (Cambodia).
Cham.cultural_notes: The Cham people are descendants of the ancient Champa Kingdom, a powerful Hindu-Buddhist civilisation that ruled central Vietnam. Today Cham are predominantly Muslim (in Vietnam and Cambodia) while some retain Hindu traditions (Bani Cham). My Son Sanctuary (UNESCO) is a remnant of Champa.
threats: Small and scattered population
Dominant languages (Vietnamese, Khmer) overwhelming daily use
Two separate communities (Vietnam and Cambodia) with different scripts
Young generation losing Cham script literacy
preservation_efforts: Cham cultural institutes in Ninh Thuan province, Vietnam
My Son UNESCO site raising awareness of Champa heritage
Community efforts to preserve Cham script and poetry
Cham traditional textile weaving (Phan Rang brocade)

Cebuano.region: Visayas Islands (Cebu, Bohol, Leyte, Samar), Mindanao
common_phrases: Kumusta ka? — How are you?
Maayo man — I'm fine
Taga-asa ka? — Where are you from?
Unsa imong ngalan? — What is your name?
Wala ko kasabot — I don't understand
Pila man kana? — How much is that?
Amping — Take care (farewell)
Cebuano.grammar_notes: Cebuano is a focus-prominent language — verbs are marked to show which argument (actor, patient, location, etc.) is in focus. It uses affixes extensively to modify meaning. Word order is relatively flexible due to the focus system.
Cebuano.cultural_notes: Cebuano speakers identify strongly with the Visayan culture. The Sinulog Festival in Cebu (January) is one of the biggest festivals in the Philippines, honouring the Santo Niño. Cebu City is considered the oldest city in the Philippines.
threats: Filipino (Tagalog-based) and English dominating formal education and media
Urban migration causing dialect mixing
Younger generation mixing Cebuano with English (Bisdak-English)
preservation_efforts: Sinulog festival preserving Cebuano cultural identity
Cebuano literature and poetry (Sugbuanon literature)
Local media (TV, radio) in Cebuano language
University of San Carlos and University of the Philippines Cebu promoting Cebuano studies

Ilocano.region: Northern Luzon (Ilocos Norte, Ilocos Sur, La Union, Pangasinan)
common_phrases: Kumustakayo? — How are you? (plural/formal)
Naimbag met laeng — I'm doing well
Tagaanoka? — Where are you from?
Ania ti naganmo? — What is your name?
Diak maawatan — I don't understand
Agyamanak unay — Thank you very much
Ilocano.grammar_notes: Ilocano, like other Philippine languages, is a focus-prominent language with verb-initial tendency. It uses a complex affix system. Ilocano has a unique sound 'ng' that appears at the start of words.
Ilocano.cultural_notes: Ilocano people are known for being hardworking and frugal (a cultural value called 'nagannak'). The Ilocos region is famous for its Spanish colonial churches (UNESCO heritage), vigan longganisa sausage, and woven fabric (abel Iloco). Many Ilocanos have migrated to Hawaii and the US mainland.
threats: Filipino (Tagalog) and English dominance in national education
Large diaspora community shifting to host country languages
Young Ilocanos mixing Ilocano with Tagalog (Taglish influence)
preservation_efforts: Ilocano language taught in some Northern Luzon schools
Bannawag (Ilocano magazine) — longest-running vernacular magazine in Philippines
Ilocano literature and poetry traditions
Ilocano Heritage Festival celebrating culture

common_phrases: Selamat datai — Welcome
Dini ko? — How are you?
Aku nyamai — I'm fine
Ari ni ko? — Where are you from?
Semina nama nuan? — What is your name?
Aku enda ngarti — I don't understand
Mupuk — Thank you
Iban.grammar_notes: Iban is related to Malay but has distinct vocabulary and grammar. It uses Subject-Verb-Object order. The language has no tones.
Iban.cultural_notes: Iban people are famous for their longhouse (Rumah Panjai) communal living. The Gawai Dayak harvest festival (June 1-2) is the biggest Iban celebration. Traditional Iban weaving (Pua Kumbu) is a sacred art form. Iban men historically underwent the bejalai (journey) tradition to prove their worth.
threats: Malay and English dominance in education and government in Malaysia
Urbanisation drawing Iban youth away from longhouse communities
Young generation preferring Malay for social mobility
Traditional knowledge and oral epics (Pantun, Timang) at risk
preservation_efforts: Gawai Dayak festival maintaining cultural identity
Iban language broadcasting on RTM Sarawak (radio/TV)
Sarawak Museum preserving Iban cultural artifacts
Pua Kumbu weaving kept alive by women artisans

common_phrases: Kokombus? — How are you?
Aiso kirarampat — I'm fine
Nokohizan kau? — Where are you from?
Onu ngaran nu? — What is your name?
Koh nobibisar oku — I don't understand
Sokogit — Thank you
Kotobian Tadau Kaamatan — Happy Harvest Festival
Kadazan-Dusun.grammar_notes: Kadazan and Dusun are closely related dialects sometimes treated as one language. They are Austronesian languages related to other Borneo languages. The language has no tones.
Kadazan-Dusun.cultural_notes: The Kaamatan (Harvest Festival) is the most important celebration, held on May 30-31. The Unduk Ngadau beauty pageant during Kaamatan celebrates the legend of Huminodun. Kadazan-Dusun have a rich oral tradition of folktales (Tuminukud).
threats: Malay and English dominance in Sabah's education and government
High degree of dialect variation making standardisation difficult
Young generation predominantly speaking Malay
Intermarriage with other groups leading to language shift
preservation_efforts: Kaamatan festival preserving cultural traditions
Kadazandusun Language Foundation (KDLF) promoting language education
Sabah Museum cultural programs
Unduk Ngadau pageant keeping cultural stories alive

common_phrases: Li jia ho? — How are you?
Ua jia ho — I'm fine
Li si lor di lang? — Where are you from?
Li kia si mi mia? — What is your name?
Ua boi hioh — I don't understand
Dor jia — Thank you
Li jia bue? — Have you eaten? (common greeting)
Teochew.grammar_notes: Teochew is a Min Nan dialect of Chinese. It has 8 tones and is tonal. Unlike Mandarin (4 tones), Teochew preserves many archaic Chinese features. It is closely related to Hokkien but not mutually intelligible.
Teochew.cultural_notes: Teochew people are a major Chinese dialect group in Southeast Asia, particularly in Cambodia and Singapore. They were major merchants and traders. Teochew cuisine (e.g., porridge, oyster omelette) is popular across SEA. The Ghost Festival and Qingming are important cultural observances.
threats: Singapore's Speak Mandarin Campaign since 1979 replacing dialect use
Young Chinese Singaporeans and Malaysians speaking English/Mandarin
Intergenerational transmission breaking down
No official status in any country
preservation_efforts: Teochew clan associations (Kongsi) maintaining cultural community
Teochew opera performances
Growing interest among younger diaspora to rediscover roots
Social media communities for Teochew language revival

common_phrases: Lí chia̍h pá bué? — Have you eaten? (common greeting)
Goa chia̍h pá ah — I've already eaten
Lí sī toh-ūi lâng? — Where are you from?
Lí kiò sím-mi̍h miâ? — What is your name?
Góa bô hiáu — I don't understand
Toh-siā — Thank you
Bô iàu-kín — Never mind / No problem
Hokkien.grammar_notes: Hokkien (Min Nan) has 7-8 tones and uses sandhi (tone changes when words combine). It is the origin language of many loanwords in Malay and Indonesian (e.g., 'teh' for tea, 'kueh' for cake, 'botak' for bald).
Hokkien.cultural_notes: Hokkien is the most influential Chinese dialect in Southeast Asia. Many Malay and Indonesian words come from Hokkien (e.g., 'teh'=tea, 'mie'=noodles, 'loteng'=upper floor). The Penang Hokkien dialect is famous for its Malay loanwords. Hokkien opera (Amoy opera) is a traditional art.
threats: Mandarin promotion in Singapore and Malaysia replacing dialects
English dominance in Philippines Chinese community
Younger generation not learning Hokkien at home
Dialect seen as 'low prestige' compared to Mandarin or English
preservation_efforts: Hokkien clan associations (Fujian Association) in SEA
Penang Hokkien cultural festivals and media
Growing social media interest in dialect revival
Hokkien popular music (Min-nan pop) from Taiwan influencing SEA

Dusun.region: Belait and Tutong districts of Brunei
common_phrases: Baapa kaamu? — How are you?
Amai oku — I'm fine
Onu ngaran nu? — What is your name?
Oku tia nobibisar — I don't understand
Terima kasih — Thank you
Dusun.grammar_notes: Brunei Dusun is related to Kadazan-Dusun of Sabah. It has been influenced by Malay due to Brunei's Malay-dominant culture. It is an Austronesian language with no tones.
Dusun.cultural_notes: Dusun people in Brunei maintain traditional harvest festivals and have converted to Islam while retaining some indigenous cultural practices. The Dusun community is a recognised indigenous (bumiputera) group in Brunei.
threats: Malay is the national language of Brunei — dominates all aspects of life
Very small speaker population
No formal education in Dusun language
Intermarriage with Malay community causing rapid language shift
preservation_efforts: Brunei government's recognition of Dusun as an indigenous group
Cultural festivals and oral tradition documentation
Academic interest from Universiti Brunei Darussalam (UBD)
Community elders preserving traditional songs and stories

common_phrases: Macam mana kabar? — How are you? (Malay-influenced)
Baik saja — Fine/OK
Dari mana? — Where are you from?
Siapa nama? — What is your name?
Tidak faham — I don't understand
Tutong.grammar_notes: Tutong is closely related to Malay and has absorbed many Malay words and grammatical structures due to Brunei's national language policy. It is an Austronesian language with Subject-Verb-Object order.
Tutong.cultural_notes: Tutong people are a recognised indigenous group in Brunei. They have unique oral traditions and traditional crafts. Like other indigenous groups in Brunei, they are predominantly Muslim and celebrate both Islamic and indigenous festivals.
threats: Malay dominance making Tutong nearly indistinguishable from regional Malay dialects for some speakers
Very small speaker population
No formal education in Tutong
Rapid assimilation into Bruneian Malay identity
preservation_efforts: Brunei government recognition of indigenous groups
Community elders maintaining oral literature
Linguistic documentation by UBD researchers
Cultural festivals in Tutong district

Tetum.status: Safe (co-official language of Timor-Leste with Portuguese)
common_phrases: Ita boot saúde diak? — How are you? (formal)
Hau saúde diak — I'm healthy/fine
Ita boot husi nebe? — Where are you from?
Ita boot nia naran saida? — What is your name?
Hau la hatene — I don't understand / I don't know
Obrigadu barak — Thank you very much
Favor — Please
Tetum.grammar_notes: Tetum has been heavily influenced by Portuguese due to centuries of Portuguese colonisation and continues to absorb Portuguese vocabulary (called Tetum Prasa/urban Tetum). The more traditional form (Tetum Terik) retains more original Austronesian features. Word order is Subject-Verb-Object.
Tetum.cultural_notes: Timor-Leste gained independence in 2002, making it one of the world's newest nations. Tetum serves as the national lingua franca among the 30+ local languages. The sacred house (Uma Lulik) is central to Timorese spiritual life. Tais (traditional woven cloth) is a national symbol.
threats: Portuguese (co-official language) competing for formal domains
English growing in business and international contexts
30+ indigenous languages competing with Tetum
Tetum Terik (traditional form) declining as Tetum Prasa (urban) dominates
preservation_efforts: Tetum is a co-official national language — supported by government policy
National University of Timor-Leste teaching in Tetum
Tais weaving tradition preservation
Uma Lulik (sacred house) cultural revival programs

common_phrases: O diak ka lae? — How are you?
Ha'u diak — I'm fine
O husi nebe? — Where are you from?
O nia naran saida? — What is your name?
Ha'u la hatene — I don't understand
Obrigadu barak — Thank you very much
Mambae.grammar_notes: Mambae is unique in Timor-Leste as it belongs to the Trans-New Guinea language family (non-Austronesian), making it linguistically distinct from most SEA languages. It has a Subject-Object-Verb tendency in traditional speech but is influenced by Tetum and Portuguese.
Mambae.cultural_notes: Mambae people live in the central highlands of Timor-Leste. They have rich animist traditions alongside Catholicism. The sacred house (Uma Lulik) is central to Mambae spiritual life. Traditional harvest ceremonies and oral poetry (dadolin) are important cultural expressions.
threats: Tetum (national language) replacing Mambae in daily life
Portuguese in formal education
Limited digital content and educational materials in Mambae
Young generation shifting to Tetum for social mobility
preservation_efforts: SIL International linguistic documentation of Mambae
Community mother-tongue education initiatives
Dadolin (traditional poetry) oral tradition preservation
Uma Lulik cultural revival in Mambae communities
